region_of_origin: East Asia (widely used in Japanese cuisine)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side serving of spring onion as a Japanese restaurant topping or garnish is approximately 30 g, equivalent to a small handful of chopped or whole stalks.

nutrient_source.title: Onions, spring or scallions (includes tops and bulb), raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #11291
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw spring onion / scallion (Allium fistulosum), whole tops and bulb, used as the primary nutrient reference. Values represent the raw edible portion per 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spring onion / scallion as a standalone food. Spring onion is a non-starchy allium vegetable with very low carbohydrate content (~7.3 g/100 g, predominantly fructooligosaccharides and fructose). GI is estimated at 15 by analogy with other non-starchy allium vegetables (onion GI ~10–15, leek GI ~15) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (15 × 2.2 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.33, rounded to 0.3. This is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (16 mg/100 g) and high potassium (276 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Spring onion is an excellent choice for individuals with hypertension due to its very low sodium content and meaningful potassium supply, which supports favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio and blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~15), low carbohydrate load, and high dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Spring onion has a negligible glycemic load per serving and its dietary fibre (2.6 g/100 g) slows glucose absorption, making it a safe and beneficial vegetable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and presence of flavonoids and organosulfur compounds
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Spring onion contains negligible saturated fat and no cholesterol, and its organosulfur compounds and quercetin content are associated with anti-inflammatory and antiplatelet effects that may support cardiovascular health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spring onion (Allium fistulosum), also known as scallion or green onion, is a slender allium vegetable with a mild, fresh flavour and crisp texture. It consists of a small white bulb and long green stalks, both of which are edible. In Japanese cuisine, spring onion (ネギ, negi) is an essential ingredient and garnish used across a wide range of dishes including gyudon (beef rice bowl), ramen, miso soup, yakitori, and soba noodles. At Sukiya, a Japanese gyudon chain, spring onion is offered as a side topping to complement beef rice bowls. Nutritionally, spring onion is a low-calorie vegetable providing approximately 32 kcal per 100 g, with modest protein (1.83 g), negligible fat (0.19 g), and moderate carbohydrate (7.34 g) largely composed of dietary fibre (2.6 g) and simple sugars. It is a good source of vitamin C (18.8 mg), vitamin A (50 mcg RAE), folate (64 mcg DFE), potassium (276 mg), and calcium (72 mg). Its very low sodium content (16 mg/100 g) and high potassium make it beneficial for blood pressure management. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low), and the glycemic load per typical 30 g serving is negligible at 0.3, making it highly suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Spring onion contains organosulfur compounds and flavonoids such as quercetin, which have demonstrated anti-inflammatory and antiplatelet properties relevant to cardiovascular health. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher.

meal_context: Side dish at Japanese fast-food restaurants

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy #11291 for raw spring onion / scallion, which is an exact match for this food. GI is estimated by analogy with closely related allium vegetables from a peer-reviewed source (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Japanese local name (ネギ) is well-established. All mandatory fields are complete.
notes: Spring onion is served as a side topping at Sukiya (Funan), a Japanese gyudon chain in Singapore, at SGD 3.00. The food_group has been corrected from 'Mixed' (input) to 'Vegetables' as spring onion is a vegetable ingredient. Cuisine is retained as Japanese per input. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy source. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy source. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant food with no B12. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy source. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.027), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.074) are reported as numeric values from USDA SR Legacy. All -1 values represent nutrients not reported or not applicable in the primary source: no -1 values remain in this record as all available USDA SR Legacy values have been populated. GI set to 15 (low) by analogy with non-starchy allium vegetables per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for spring onion as a standalone food exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 30 g serving calculated as (15 × 2.2 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.33, rounded to 0.3.
